schools.summary: Forbids rash, censorious, unjust judgment (Rom 2:1; Lk 6:37), not the magistrate's office or a necessary opinion of evil; 'critic' from krinō.
Be not a bitter judge but a physician; the ban lies on those full of great evils who judge small ones; harmonized with 'reprove, rebuke.'
Put the best interpretation on doubtful acts; plain evils may be judged, indifferent ones not.
A cure for the natural disease of probing others' faults while overlooking our own.

sides.position: no; only rash/censorious judgment, not lawful or charitable discernment

outliers.note: Takes 'judge not' of judging God concerning his promises, where any judgment at all is sin.

schools.summary: A common Jewish proverb ('in the measure a man metes...') adopted by Christ; a retributive law touching both God's and men's judgment of us.
We choose whether God shall be severe or merciful; the merciless must expect judgment without mercy.
karphos (splinter/chaff) vs dokos (rafter/log); a current proverb (Tarphon, Arabic, Burns): keen to others' small faults, blind to our own great ones.
Beam vs mote = hatred vs anger (Augustine); the teacher's sin the beam; the unbeliever's distrust the beam (Hilary).
The hypocrite's double fault: sharp-sighted to others' sins, ingenious in excusing his own.
An expression of astonishment at the censorious man's effrontery ('how wilt thou have the face to say'); a known rabbinic proverb (Tarphon).
Rebukes those who, not content to mark faults, reprove in a magisterial way while guilty themselves.
'See clearly' (diablepseis, look through) vs gazing; first remove your beam, then you can rightly help the brother — no hypocrisy, clear sight.
'Hypocrite' fits one free in reproving others' sins while covering his own, to be thought holier than he is.

outliers.note: Note here is mis-attached, on the Lord's Prayer rather than the beam and mote.

schools.summary: 'The holy' = sacrificial flesh (Lev 22; Exod 22:31), the 'pearls' the gospel's truths; dogs/swine = malignant despisers; note the transposition (dogs rend, swine trample).
The holy = what is impious to corrupt; priests withhold spiritual things (even baptism) from hardened enemies of the faith.
Not against wide preaching (Mk 16:15) but a charge to reserve heavenly wisdom from profane despisers once they prove hardened.

sides.position: sacrificial flesh / holy things, figuring the gospel
baptism and the spiritual graces withheld from the unworthy

schools.summary: Ask/seek/knock = rising earnestness (want, loss, earnestness); persevering prayer with implied right conditions (1 Jn 5:14); courage and wisdom sought in prayer.
Asking gains soul-health, seeking the truth, knocking actual possession; faith, hope, love (Gloss).
No superfluous words but perseverance to rouse us; the promises move us, for prayer without faith is empty.

outliers.note: Joins 'seek and find' to the merchant seeking the one pearl, Christ, counting all else loss (Phil 3:8).

schools.summary: Prayer 'always heard after one manner or other'; the promise as binding on God as the law on man; God no respecter of persons (Ps 81:10; Jn 16:24).
God is of himself ready to listen, his riches at our command; the only failure is the slothful failure to ask.
A homely Galilean image (bread/stone); stones can look like loaves (Mt 4:3); Seneca's 'stony bread' for a grudging gift.
Men 'evil' as lovers of this age; loaf = the word/knowledge of God, fish = faith of baptism, against stone and serpent.
Argument less-to-greater: a father's love is a drop of God's goodness (Eph 3:15); what then from the ocean?
Serpent resembles a fish (eel); fish/bread the staple of Galilee; Luke adds egg/scorpion — all show the absurdity of a father mocking a child's request.
'Evil' recognizes depravity yet not total: natural affection makes human fatherhood a parable of God's; 'good things' = spiritual gifts / the Holy Spirit (Lk 11:13).
Christ names 'good things' to curb improper desires: submit our wishes to God's will, who gives only what is advantageous.
Paternal love called 'evil' only by comparison with God's superabundant goodness.

outliers.note: Stray fragment on the angels' vision of God, unrelated to the verse.

schools.summary: The Golden Rule sums the Law and Prophets; the negative form known to Hillel/Tobit/Confucius, Christ's positive form distinctive; guarded against private misuse.
All commands summed in one precept (Cyprian); owning our equal (Augustine); returning good for evil (Gregory).
The 'therefore' resumes 'judge not'; do for neighbour what you would have, the condition of being heard in prayer.
The connective 'therefore' is a proverb-particle; the rule is the law of justice subjoined to the law of prayer.
'Strait' (narrow) not 'straight'; the Two Ways (Deut 30:19; Didache; Tablet of Cebes); rabbinic public vs private road widths.
The narrow way is yet light and pleasant, its toil borne for the good end of life (cf. the easy yoke).
The broad way needs no search; many find the narrow way yet desert it midway for the world's pleasures.
The strait gate is Christ the door; the disciple must walk a thorny path, soothed by a joyful reward.
'Narrow' = hemmed in like a mountain gorge; first appearance of 'life' summing the kingdom's blessedness; 'how' vs 'because' reading; the few vs the many.
The way hedged with afflictions, like the strait place where Balaam's angel stood, yet alone leads to life.
False prophets = false teachers / claimants to inspiration; sheep's clothing hides ravening wolves (2 Cor 11:13; 1 Jn 4:1); known by fruits.
Specially heretics, cloaked in chastity and fasting yet poisoned within; the hypocrite strips off the sheepskin under trial (Gregory).
Aimed chiefly at corrupt-living teachers wearing virtue's face, since heretics may show a good life but these never.
The Church exposed to impositions; Satan ever laying snares; perhaps a glance at the Scribes and Pharisees.

debates.question: Are heretics or corrupt-living teachers chiefly meant?

sides.position: corrupt-living teachers wearing a face of virtue

schools.summary: Fruits = works of any kind, the tongue of the heart; a tree judged by fruit not foliage (Mt 12:33); epignōsesthe, fully know.
The mark saves us from holding all teachers suspect; Gill takes the fruits chiefly as their doctrines and effects.
A plain test for the weakest: true prophets convert sinners, false do not (Lk 6:44).

sides.position: works / life and conduct (the ethical reading)

schools.summary: The tree is good before its fruit; works manifest but do not make a man good; so a good preacher delivers sound doctrine.
'Corrupt' = useless by nature, not vitiated; profession with an ungodly life is imposture (Lk 6:43).
The goodness/badness respects doctrine rather than personal character: a bad man may preach good doctrine and convert sinners.

debates.question: Does the tree-figure judge doctrine or personal character?

sides.position: chiefly doctrine (a bad man may preach truth)
the good heart and holy life

outliers.note: Note here is mis-attached, on treasures and moths (Mt 6:19).

schools.summary: A Spirit-led teacher cannot knowingly deliver doctrine against God's grace, Christ, or the saints' experience.
Love to God and man is the root; against antinomianism that lets the good tree bear bad fruit.
Again refers the goodness to doctrine; none are true prophets who do not convert, though not all who convert are true.
Echoes the Baptist's warning (Mt 3:10), linking Christ's teaching to the forerunner's; the excision is present and the curse now on false teachers.
How dreadful the teacher who has brought no sinners to God.
Cyril presses it on the hypocrite (Simon Magus): the axe is at the root, the Judge inexorable.
A deliberate repetition fixing the rule; epignōsesthe = full, decisive knowledge (1 Cor 13:12); no innocent sterility — no fruit is to have evil.
Try men by their doctrines and the doctrines by Scripture; ordinarily as men are, so are their doctrines.
Sharp 'not...but': the doer of the Father's will enters, not the mere confessor; confirms the ethical reading of 'fruits' (Jn 7:17).
Sound doctrine destroyed by evil works avails nothing (Titus 1:16); 'say' = the will (reconciling 1 Cor 12:3); charity over mighty works (Gregory).
Censures the Jews who rest in dogmas; 'the will of my Father' (in condescension) — yet the Son's will is the Father's; Christ shows himself Judge.
Embraces all hypocrites, chiefly pretended teachers without the fear of God.

outliers.note: Stray fragment on baptism of water and Spirit, unrelated to the verse.

schools.summary: Christ's marvellous claim to be Judge 'in that day'; miracles no proof of piety; ou expects 'yes' — they really prophesied, yet are unmasked.
Christ summons hypocrites to his judgment-seat; the day he cleanses his barn, parting chaff from wheat.
'I never knew you' = never approved/owned (not once-known-then-rejected); they were never true Christians; 'profess' (homologēsō) the public word of Mt 10:32.
Against the hypocrites' boasting Christ openly confesses their profession false; he never reckoned them his own; condemnation falls on workers of lawlessness.

outliers.note: Note here is mis-attached, on serving two masters (Mt 6:24).

schools.summary: Hearing joined to doing; the deep foundation on rock (Lk 6:48); Palestinian flash-floods; rabbinic parallel of works vs knowledge (Eleazar).
Allegory: the rock = the faith/the Church; storms = superstition, rumours, lust; none fears them who does the command.
Two houses of the same appearance; the unfounded falls, the founded stands; a vain profession wants a foundation.
Rain/floods/winds = Satan's temptations, the world's persecutions, heart's corruptions, false doctrine; the house on Christ stands (Ps 92:13).
'Was founded' (past perfect): it had been built on rock and stood; eastern torrents melt sun-dried brick houses; three trials of believers.
The fatal flaw is hearing without doing; sand cannot hold in the storm; hearing sermons is dangerous without practice (cf. Mt 5:19).
The foolish hearer rests on external hearing or speculative knowledge without practice; mere talk of Christ is self-deception.
The unfounded cannot stand against temptation, persecution, lust, false doctrine, or divine wrath; the fall inevitable and great.
Bengal fishermen's sand huts swept away in a night; the testing fire and dreadful fall (1 Cor 3:13; Heb 10:26).
Astonished at his teaching, its manner more than substance; exeplēssonto = struck out of themselves; 'ended these sayings' implies one continuous discourse.
Wonder vs faith: their admiration touched Christ's glory, not their belief; yet his power drew them to follow him down the mount.
The wondering multitude = the simple folk; Augustine harmonizes Matthew's mount-sermon with Luke's plain.
An unwonted majesty in his doctrine drew the minds of men.
The scribes borrowed authority from tradition ('the wise men say'); Jesus spoke with the authority of truth and the Spirit; the 'I say unto you' and the absence of cited rabbis.
As God and Lord of Moses he freely added to or changed the Law; not 'thus saith the Lord' like the prophets but his own word.
He taught as the great Lawgiver, with the power of the Spirit, not as the scribes who expounded another's law lifelessly.
